title: Nebulae

infobox.value: ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language") for 'cloud, fog';[[1]](#cite_note-1) pl. **nebulae** or **nebulas**)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) is a distinct luminescent part of [interstellar medium](/wiki/Interstellar_medium "Interstellar medium"), which can consist of ionized, neutral, or molecular [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") and also [cosmic dust](/wiki/Cosmic_dust "Cosmic dust"). Nebulae are often star-forming regions, such as the [Pillars of Creation](/wiki/Pillars_of_Creation "Pillars of Creation") in the [Eagle Nebula](/wiki/Eagle_Nebula "Eagle Nebula"). In these regions, the formations of gas, dust, and other materials "clump" together to form denser regions, which attract further matter and eventually become dense enough to form [stars](/wiki/Star "Star"). The remaining material is then thought to form [planets](/wiki/Planet "Planet") and other [planetary system](/wiki/Planetary_system "Planetary system") objects.

For other uses, see [Nebula (disambiguation)](/wiki/Nebula_(disambiguation) "Nebula (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Trifid_Nebula_by_Deddy_Dayag.jpg/500px-Trifid_Nebula_by_Deddy_Dayag.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trifid_Nebula_by_Deddy_Dayag.jpg)

True color image of the [Trifid Nebula](/wiki/Trifid_Nebula "Trifid Nebula"), showing complex gas and plasma structure

A **nebula** ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language") for 'cloud, fog';[[1]](#cite_note-1) pl. **nebulae** or **nebulas**)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) is a distinct luminescent part of [interstellar medium](/wiki/Interstellar_medium "Interstellar medium"), which can consist of ionized, neutral, or molecular [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") and also [cosmic dust](/wiki/Cosmic_dust "Cosmic dust"). Nebulae are often star-forming regions, such as the [Pillars of Creation](/wiki/Pillars_of_Creation "Pillars of Creation") in the [Eagle Nebula](/wiki/Eagle_Nebula "Eagle Nebula"). In these regions, the formations of gas, dust, and other materials "clump" together to form denser regions, which attract further matter and eventually become dense enough to form [stars](/wiki/Star "Star"). The remaining material is then thought to form [planets](/wiki/Planet "Planet") and other [planetary system](/wiki/Planetary_system "Planetary system") objects.

Most nebulae are of vast size; some are hundreds of [light-years](/wiki/Light-year "Light-year") in diameter. A nebula that is visible to the human eye from [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") would appear larger, but no brighter, from close by.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The [Orion Nebula](/wiki/Orion_Nebula "Orion Nebula"), the brightest nebula in the sky and occupying an area twice the angular diameter of the full [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon"), can be viewed with the naked eye but was missed by early astronomers.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Although denser than the space surrounding them, most nebulae are far less dense than any [vacuum](/wiki/Vacuum "Vacuum") created on Earth (105 to 107 molecules per cubic centimeter) – a nebular cloud the size of the [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth") would have a total mass of only a few [kilograms](/wiki/Kilogram "Kilogram"). Earth's air has a density of approximately 1019 molecules per cubic centimeter; by contrast, the densest nebulae can have densities of 104 molecules per cubic centimeter. Many nebulae are visible due to fluorescence caused by embedded hot stars, while others are so diffused that they can be detected only with long exposures and special filters. Some nebulae are variably illuminated by [T Tauri](/wiki/T_Tauri_star "T Tauri star") variable stars.

Originally, the term "nebula" was used to describe any diffused [astronomical object](/wiki/Astronomical_object "Astronomical object"), including [galaxies](/wiki/Galaxy "Galaxy") beyond the [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way"). The [Andromeda Galaxy](/wiki/Andromeda_Galaxy "Andromeda Galaxy"), for instance, was once referred to as the *Andromeda Nebula* (and [spiral galaxies](/wiki/Spiral_galaxies "Spiral galaxies") in general as "spiral nebulae") before the true nature of galaxies was confirmed in the early 20th century by [Vesto Slipher](/wiki/Vesto_Slipher "Vesto Slipher"), [Edwin Hubble](/wiki/Edwin_Hubble "Edwin Hubble"), and others. Edwin Hubble discovered that most nebulae are associated with stars and illuminated by starlight. He also helped categorize nebulae based on the type of light spectra they produced.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Observational history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nebula&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Observational history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Hs-2009-25-e-full.jpg/250px-Hs-2009-25-e-full.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hs-2009-25-e-full.jpg)

Portion of the [Carina Nebula](/wiki/Carina_Nebula "Carina Nebula")

Around 150 AD, [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") recorded, in books VII–VIII of his *[Almagest](/wiki/Almagest "Almagest")*, five stars that appeared nebulous. He also noted a region of nebulosity between the [constellations](/wiki/Constellation "Constellation") [Ursa Major](/wiki/Ursa_Major "Ursa Major") and [Leo](/wiki/Leo_(constellation) "Leo (constellation)") that was not associated with any [star](/wiki/Star "Star").[[9]](#cite_note-9) The first true nebula, as distinct from a [star cluster](/wiki/Star_cluster "Star cluster"), was mentioned by the [Muslim Persian astronomer](/wiki/Astronomy_in_the_medieval_Islamic_world "Astronomy in the medieval Islamic world") [Abd al-Rahman al-Sufi](/wiki/Abd_al-Rahman_al-Sufi "Abd al-Rahman al-Sufi") in his *[Book of Fixed Stars](/wiki/Book_of_Fixed_Stars "Book of Fixed Stars")* (964).[[10]](#cite_note-Jones-10) He noted "a little cloud" where the [Andromeda Galaxy](/wiki/Andromeda_Galaxy "Andromeda Galaxy") is located.[[11]](#cite_note-rasqj25-11) He also cataloged the [Omicron Velorum](/wiki/Omicron_Velorum_Cluster "Omicron Velorum Cluster") star cluster as a "nebulous star" and other nebulous objects, such as [Brocchi's Cluster](/wiki/Brocchi%27s_Cluster "Brocchi's Cluster").[[10]](#cite_note-Jones-10) The [supernovas](/wiki/Supernovas "Supernovas") that created the [Crab Nebula](/wiki/Crab_Nebula "Crab Nebula"), [SN 1054](/wiki/SN_1054 "SN 1054"), was observed by Arabic and [Chinese astronomers](/wiki/Chinese_astronomy "Chinese astronomy") in 1054.[[12]](#cite_note-Lundmark-12)[[13]](#cite_note-Mayall-13)

In 1610, [Nicolas-Claude Fabri de Peiresc](/wiki/Nicolas-Claude_Fabri_de_Peiresc "Nicolas-Claude Fabri de Peiresc") discovered the [Orion Nebula](/wiki/Orion_Nebula "Orion Nebula") using a telescope. This nebula was also observed by [Johann Baptist Cysat](/wiki/Johann_Baptist_Cysat "Johann Baptist Cysat") in 1618. However, the first detailed study of the Orion Nebula was not performed until 1659 by [Christiaan Huygens](/wiki/Christiaan_Huygens "Christiaan Huygens"), who also believed he was the first person to discover this nebulosity.[[11]](#cite_note-rasqj25-11)

In 1715, [Edmond Halley](/wiki/Edmond_Halley "Edmond Halley") published a list of six nebulae.[[14]](#cite_note-14) This number steadily increased during the century, with [Jean-Philippe de Cheseaux](/wiki/Jean-Philippe_de_Cheseaux "Jean-Philippe de Cheseaux") compiling a list of 20 (including eight not previously known) in 1746. From 1751 to 1753, [Nicolas-Louis de Lacaille](/wiki/Nicolas-Louis_de_Lacaille "Nicolas-Louis de Lacaille") cataloged 42 nebulae from the [Cape of Good Hope](/wiki/Cape_of_Good_Hope "Cape of Good Hope"), most of which were previously unknown. [Charles Messier](/wiki/Charles_Messier "Charles Messier") then compiled a catalog of 103 "nebulae" (now called [Messier objects](/wiki/Messier_object "Messier object"), which included what are now known to be galaxies) by 1781; his interest was detecting [comets](/wiki/Comet "Comet"), and these were objects that might be mistaken for them.[[15]](#cite_note-hoskin2005-15)

The number of nebulae was then greatly increased by the efforts of [William Herschel](/wiki/William_Herschel "William Herschel") and his sister, [Caroline Herschel](/wiki/Caroline_Herschel "Caroline Herschel"). Their *Catalogue of One Thousand New Nebulae and Clusters of Stars*[[16]](#cite_note-16) was published in 1786. A second catalog of a thousand was published in 1789, and the third and final catalog of 510 appeared in 1802. During much of their work, William Herschel believed that these nebulae were merely unresolved clusters of stars. In 1790, however, he discovered a star surrounded by nebulosity and concluded that this was a true nebulosity rather than a more distant cluster.[[15]](#cite_note-hoskin2005-15)

Beginning in 1864, [William Huggins](/wiki/William_Huggins "William Huggins") examined the spectra of about 70 nebulae. He found that roughly a third of them had the [emission spectrum](/wiki/Emission_spectrum "Emission spectrum") of a [gas](/wiki/Gas "Gas"). The rest showed a continuous spectrum and were thus thought to consist of a mass of stars.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-struve37-18) A third category was added in 1912 when [Vesto Slipher](/wiki/Vesto_Slipher "Vesto Slipher") showed that the spectrum of the nebula that surrounded the star [Merope](/wiki/Merope_(star) "Merope (star)") matched the spectra of the [Pleiades](/wiki/Pleiades "Pleiades") [open cluster](/wiki/Open_cluster "Open cluster"). Thus, the nebula radiates by reflected star light.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In 1923, following the [Great Debate](/wiki/Great_Debate_(astronomy) "Great Debate (astronomy)"), it became clear that many "nebulae" were in fact galaxies far from the [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way").

Slipher and [Edwin Hubble](/wiki/Edwin_Hubble "Edwin Hubble") continued to collect the spectra from many different nebulae, finding 29 that showed emission spectra and 33 that had the continuous spectra of star light.[[18]](#cite_note-struve37-18) In 1922, Hubble announced that nearly all nebulae are associated with stars and that their illumination comes from star light. He also discovered that the emission spectrum nebulae are nearly always associated with stars having spectral classifications of B or hotter (including all [O-type main sequence stars](/wiki/O-type_main_sequence_star "O-type main sequence star")), while nebulae with continuous spectra appear with cooler stars.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Both Hubble and [Henry Norris Russell](/wiki/Henry_Norris_Russell "Henry Norris Russell") concluded that the nebulae surrounding the hotter stars are transformed in some manner.[[18]](#cite_note-struve37-18)

## Formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nebula&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Formation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Nursery_of_New_Stars_-_GPN-2000-000972.jpg/250px-Nursery_of_New_Stars_-_GPN-2000-000972.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nursery_of_New_Stars_-_GPN-2000-000972.jpg)

[NGC 604](/wiki/NGC_604 "NGC 604"), a nebula in the [Triangulum Galaxy](/wiki/Triangulum_Galaxy "Triangulum Galaxy")

There are a variety of formation mechanisms for the different types of nebulae. Some nebulae form from gas that is already in the [interstellar medium](/wiki/Interstellar_medium "Interstellar medium") while others are produced by stars. Examples of the former case are [giant molecular clouds](/wiki/Giant_molecular_clouds "Giant molecular clouds"), the coldest, densest phase of interstellar gas, which can form by the cooling and condensation of more diffuse gas. Examples of the latter case are planetary nebulae formed from material shed by a star in late stages of its [stellar evolution](/wiki/Stellar_evolution "Stellar evolution").

[Star-forming regions](/wiki/Star-forming_region "Star-forming region") are a class of emission nebula associated with giant molecular clouds. These form as a molecular cloud collapses under its own weight, producing stars. Massive stars may form in the center, and their [ultraviolet radiation](/wiki/Ultraviolet_radiation "Ultraviolet radiation") [ionizes](/wiki/Ion "Ion") the surrounding gas, making it visible at optical [wavelengths](/wiki/Wavelength "Wavelength"). The region of ionized hydrogen surrounding the massive stars is known as an [H II region](/wiki/H_II_region "H II region") while the shells of neutral hydrogen surrounding the H II region are known as [photodissociation region](/wiki/Photodissociation_region "Photodissociation region"). Examples of star-forming regions are the [Orion Nebula](/wiki/Orion_Nebula "Orion Nebula"), the [Rosette Nebula](/wiki/Rosette_Nebula "Rosette Nebula") and the [Omega Nebula](/wiki/Omega_Nebula "Omega Nebula"). Feedback from star-formation, in the form of supernova explosions of massive stars, stellar winds or ultraviolet radiation from massive stars, or outflows from low-mass stars may disrupt the cloud, destroying the nebula after several million years.

Other nebulae form as the result of [supernova](/wiki/Supernova "Supernova") explosions; the death throes of massive, short-lived stars. The materials thrown off from the supernova explosion are then ionized by the energy and the compact object that its core produces. One of the best examples of this is the [Crab Nebula](/wiki/Crab_Nebula "Crab Nebula"), in [Taurus](/wiki/Taurus_(constellation) "Taurus (constellation)"). The supernova event was recorded in the year 1054 and is labeled [SN 1054](/wiki/SN_1054 "SN 1054"). The compact object that was created after the explosion lies in the center of the Crab Nebula and its core is now a [neutron star](/wiki/Neutron_star "Neutron star").

Still other nebulae form as [planetary nebulae](/wiki/Planetary_nebulae "Planetary nebulae"). This is the final stage of a low-mass star's life, like Earth's Sun. Stars with a mass up to 8–10 solar masses evolve into [red giants](/wiki/Red_giant "Red giant") and slowly lose their outer layers during pulsations in their atmospheres. When a star has lost enough material, its temperature increases and the [ultraviolet radiation](/wiki/Ultraviolet_radiation "Ultraviolet radiation") it emits can [ionize](/wiki/Ion "Ion") the surrounding nebula that it has thrown off. The Sun will produce a planetary nebula and its core will remain behind in the form of a [white dwarf](/wiki/White_dwarf "White dwarf").

### Classical types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nebula&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Classical types")]

Objects named nebulae belong to four major groups. Before their nature was understood, [galaxies](/wiki/Galaxy "Galaxy") ("spiral nebulae") and [star clusters](/wiki/Star_cluster "Star cluster") too distant to be resolved as stars were also classified as nebulae, but no longer are.

* [H II regions](/wiki/H_II_region "H II region"), large diffuse nebulae containing ionized hydrogen
* [Planetary nebulae](/wiki/Planetary_nebula "Planetary nebula")
* [Supernova remnants](/wiki/Supernova_remnant "Supernova remnant") (e.g., Crab Nebula)
* [Dark nebulae](/wiki/Dark_nebula "Dark nebula")

Not all cloud-like structures are nebulae; [Herbig–Haro objects](/wiki/Herbig%E2%80%93Haro_object "Herbig–Haro object") are an example.

### Flux Nebulae

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nebula&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Flux Nebulae")]

Main article: [Integrated Flux Nebula](/wiki/Integrated_Flux_Nebula "Integrated Flux Nebula")

This section is an excerpt from [Integrated Flux Nebula](/wiki/Integrated_Flux_Nebula "Integrated Flux Nebula").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Integrated_Flux_Nebula&action=edit)]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/M81_M82_Garland_Galaxy.png/250px-M81_M82_Garland_Galaxy.png)](/wiki/File:M81_M82_Garland_Galaxy.png)

M81 (upper right), M82 (left), and [NGC 3077](/wiki/NGC_3077 "NGC 3077") (bottom right) with a large complex of IFN

[Integrated Flux Nebulae (IFN)](/wiki/Integrated_Flux_Nebula "Integrated Flux Nebula") are a relatively recently[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] identified astronomical phenomenon. In contrast to the typical and well-known gaseous nebulae within the plane of the [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way") [galaxy](/wiki/Galaxy "Galaxy"), IFNs lie beyond the main body of the galaxy.

The term was coined by [Steve Mandel](/wiki/Steve_Mandel "Steve Mandel"), who defined them as "high galactic latitude nebulae that are illuminated not by a single star (as most nebulae in the plane of the [galaxy](/wiki/Galaxy "Galaxy") are) but by the energy from the integrated flux of all the stars in the Milky Way. As a result, these nebulae are incredibly faint, taking hours of exposure to capture. These nebulae clouds, an important component of the interstellar medium, are composed of dust particles, hydrogen, carbon monoxide, and some other elements."[[22]](#cite_note-Integrated_Flux_Nebula_:0-22) They are particularly prominent in the direction of both the north and south [celestial poles](/wiki/Celestial_pole "Celestial pole"). The vast nebula close to the south celestial pole is MW9, commonly known as the South Celestial Serpent.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Diffuse nebulae

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nebula&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Diffuse nebulae")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Carina_Nebula_by_ESO.jpg/250px-Carina_Nebula_by_ESO.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carina_Nebula_by_ESO.jpg)

The Carina Nebula is an example of a diffuse nebula

Most nebulae can be described as diffuse nebulae, which means that they are extended and contain no well-defined boundaries.[[24]](#cite_note-Messier-24) Diffuse nebulae can be divided into [emission nebulae](/wiki/Emission_nebula "Emission nebula"), [reflection nebulae](/wiki/Reflection_nebula "Reflection nebula") and [dark nebulae](/wiki/Dark_nebula "Dark nebula").

Visible light nebulae may be divided into emission nebulae, which emit [spectral line](/wiki/Spectral_line "Spectral line") radiation from excited or [ionized](/wiki/Ion "Ion") gas (mostly ionized [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen"));[[25]](#cite_note-shu1982-25) they are often called [H II regions](/wiki/H_II_region "H II region"), (H II referring to ionized hydrogen), and reflection nebulae which are visible primarily due to the light they reflect.

Reflection nebulae themselves do not emit significant amounts of visible light, but are near stars and reflect light from them.[[25]](#cite_note-shu1982-25) Similar nebulae not illuminated by stars do not exhibit visible radiation, but may be detected as opaque clouds blocking light from luminous objects behind them; they are called [dark nebulae](/wiki/Dark_nebula "Dark nebula").[[25]](#cite_note-shu1982-25)

Although these nebulae have different visibility at optical wavelengths, they are all bright sources of [infrared](/wiki/Infrared "Infrared") emission, chiefly from [dust](/wiki/Cosmic_dust "Cosmic dust") within the nebulae.[[25]](#cite_note-shu1982-25)

### Planetary nebulae

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nebula&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Planetary nebulae")]

Main article: [Planetary nebula](/wiki/Planetary_nebula "Planetary nebula")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/A_hazy_nebula.jpg/250px-A_hazy_nebula.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_hazy_nebula.jpg)

The [Oyster Nebula](/wiki/NGC_1501 "NGC 1501") is a [planetary nebula](/wiki/Planetary_nebula "Planetary nebula") located in the constellation of [Camelopardalis](/wiki/Camelopardalis "Camelopardalis")

Planetary nebulae are the remnants of the final stages of stellar evolution for mid-mass stars (varying in size between 0.5-~8 solar masses). Evolved [asymptotic giant branch](/wiki/Asymptotic_giant_branch "Asymptotic giant branch") stars expel their outer layers outwards due to strong stellar winds, thus forming gaseous shells while leaving behind the star's core in the form of a [white dwarf](/wiki/White_dwarf "White dwarf").[[25]](#cite_note-shu1982-25) Radiation from the hot white dwarf excites the expelled gases, producing emission nebulae with spectra similar to those of emission nebulae found in [star formation](/wiki/Star_formation "Star formation") regions.[[25]](#cite_note-shu1982-25) They are [H II regions](/wiki/H_II_region "H II region"), because mostly hydrogen is ionized, but planetary are denser and more compact than nebulae found in star formation regions.[[25]](#cite_note-shu1982-25)

Planetary nebulae were given their own name by the first astronomical observers who were initially unable to distinguish them from planets, which were of more interest to them. The Sun is expected to spawn a planetary nebula about 12 billion years after its formation.[[26]](#cite_note-s-26)

#### Protoplanetary nebulae

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nebula&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Protoplanetary nebulae")]

Main article: [Protoplanetary nebula](/wiki/Protoplanetary_nebula "Protoplanetary nebula")

This section is an excerpt from [Protoplanetary nebula](/wiki/Protoplanetary_nebula "Protoplanetary nebula").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Protoplanetary_nebula&action=edit)]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Westbrook_Nebula.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Westbrook_Nebula.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Westbrook_Nebula.tif)

The [Westbrook Nebula](/wiki/Westbrook_Nebula "Westbrook Nebula"), a protoplanetary nebula.

A [protoplanetary nebula](/wiki/Protoplanetary_nebula "Protoplanetary nebula") or preplanetary nebula[[27]](#cite_note-Protoplanetary_nebula_Sahai_&_Sanchez_Contreras_et_al_2005-27) (PPN, plural PPNe) is an [astronomical object](/wiki/Astronomical_object "Astronomical object") which is at the short-lived episode during a [star](/wiki/Star "Star")'s rapid [evolution](/wiki/Stellar_evolution "Stellar evolution") between the late [asymptotic giant branch](/wiki/Asymptotic_giant_branch "Asymptotic giant branch") (LAGB)[[a]](#endnote_Anone) phase and the subsequent [planetary nebula](/wiki/Planetary_nebula "Planetary nebula") (PN) phase. A PPN emits strongly in [infrared](/wiki/Infrared "Infrared") radiation, and is a kind of [reflection nebula](/wiki/Reflection_nebula "Reflection nebula"). It is the second-from-the-last high-luminosity evolution phase in the life cycle of intermediate-mass stars (1–8 [M☉](/wiki/Solar_mass "Solar mass")).[[28]](#cite_note-Protoplanetary_nebula_Kastner2005-28): 469

### Supernova remnants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nebula&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Supernova remnants")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Crab_Nebula.jpg/250px-Crab_Nebula.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crab_Nebula.jpg)

The [Crab Nebula](/wiki/Crab_Nebula "Crab Nebula"), an example of a [supernova remnant](/wiki/Supernova_remnant "Supernova remnant")

A [supernova](/wiki/Supernova "Supernova") occurs when a high-mass star reaches the end of its life. When [nuclear fusion](/wiki/Nuclear_fusion "Nuclear fusion") in the core of the star stops, the star collapses. The gas falling inward either rebounds or gets so strongly heated that it expands outwards from the core, thus causing the star to explode.[[25]](#cite_note-shu1982-25) The expanding shell of gas forms a [supernova remnant](/wiki/Supernova_remnant "Supernova remnant"), a special [diffuse nebula](/wiki/Diffuse_nebula "Diffuse nebula").[[25]](#cite_note-shu1982-25) Although much of the optical and [X-ray](/wiki/X-ray "X-ray") emission from supernova remnants originates from ionized gas, a great amount of the [radio](/wiki/Radio_wave "Radio wave") emission is a form of non-thermal emission called [synchrotron emission](/wiki/Synchrotron_emission "Synchrotron emission").[[25]](#cite_note-shu1982-25) This emission originates from high-velocity [electrons](/wiki/Electron "Electron") oscillating within [magnetic fields](/wiki/Magnetic_field "Magnetic field").
